title: Boys and Girls

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-04-probability-theory.md

setup.narrative: Two related questions about a family with two children. Assume each child is independently a boy or girl with probability 1/2.
rules: Sample space for two children (in birth order): {(B, B), (B, G), (G, B), (G, G)}, each equally likely.
Part A: Ms. Jackson is invited to a dinner for working mothers WITH AT LEAST ONE SON.
Part B: You see Ms. Parker walking with ONE OF her two children, who happens to be a boy.
setup.question: In each part: what is the probability both children are boys?

learningObjectives: Recognize that the conditioning event is DIFFERENT in Parts A and B.
Compute P(both boys | at least one boy) = 1/3 by enumerating the conditional sample space.
Compute P(both boys | this specific child is a boy) = 1/2 by independence.

stages.label: Part A: identify the conditioning event
stages.prompt: What is the event we're conditioning on in Part A?

options.text: 'At least one of the two children is a boy.'  This eliminates only the (G, G) outcome.
'A specific child (say the older) is a boy.'  Eliminates (G, G) and (G, B).

stages.explanation: The dinner invites mothers with at least one son. The information is about the family unit, not any specific child. Only (G, G) is incompatible. Three outcomes remain equally likely: (B, B), (B, G), (G, B).

stages.label: Part A: compute P(both boys | at least one boy)
stages.prompt: Of the 3 surviving outcomes (B,B), (B,G), (G,B), how many are 'both boys'?  Compute the conditional probability.

stages.label: Part B: identify the conditioning event
stages.prompt: Now you see Ms. Parker with ONE of her two children, and that child is a boy. What's the conditioning event?

options.text: 'A specific child (the one walking with her) is a boy.' This is INDEPENDENT of the other child's gender.
Same as Part A: 'at least one boy.'

stages.explanation: You're not learning about the family in aggregate -- you're seeing one specific child. The OTHER child is unobserved and remains 50/50 boy/girl by independence.

stages.label: Commit both answers
stages.prompt: What are P(both boys) for Part A and Part B?
stages.explanation: Different conditioning events, different answers. Part A: 1/3 (eliminate only (G,G); 1 of 3 survivors is BB). Part B: 1/2 (the OTHER child is independent and equally likely to be either).

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: Suppose Ms. Parker says 'my older child is a boy.' Is this Part A or Part B in disguise?
Why does the precise wording 'at least one' vs 'one specific' matter so much? Construct a scenario where you might MISTAKENLY apply the wrong condition.
Generalize Part A to a family of THREE children: P(all three boys | at least one boy)?
